abstract:
This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the spatial structure and development levels of Polish agriculture, primarily utilizing data from the 2010 National Agricultural Census. The authors categorize the internal characteristics of the agricultural sector into six diagnostic groups: land quality and use, agrarian structure, socio-demographic features, technical equipment, production structure, and production-economic efficiency. These factors form the basis of a synthetic index used to evaluate the general level of agriculture across 314 regional offices of the Agency for Restructuring and Modernisation of Agriculture (ARiMR). 

The findings indicate significant spatial disparities at both the provincial and district levels. These patterns are analyzed in relation to four external determinants: natural conditions, historical-political legacy, urbanization levels, and the absorption of Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) funds. The results demonstrate that while the impact of natural environmental quality is relatively minor, non-natural factors—particularly historical boundaries and the effectiveness of EU fund utilization—play a dominant role in shaping the modern agricultural landscape. Furthermore, the study identifies specific problem areas, particularly in south-eastern Poland (Małopolskie and Podkarpackie provinces), where fragmented agrarian structures and low modernization levels continue to limit development, suggesting that historical determinants remain the most influential factor in the spatial differentiation of Polish farming.
